So this is the fix for that py3status crash bug I was talking about. To see what I'm talking about we have to distinguish between two separate things:

(1) Refreshing the module
(2) Fetching events 

Doing (1) as often as we want is no problem. However, if (2) is done successively very quickly, the module will crash. So I have to decouple (1) from (2), meaning if  we press `button_open` or `button_toggle`, we want to refresh the module but NOT fetch events. Why? Because if you hit say `button_toggle` multiple times in a second without decoupling the two, this will try to fetch events over and over and eventually py3status will crash. Nothing gets logged when this happens, but I was able to determine that it has to do with trying to fetch events again before the last fetch was incomplete...so refreshing events too fast is bad. 

So the solution I did has two elements:

One, we don't update events for `button_toggle` or `button_open`. This is what `self.no_update` is for. We set it to True at the start of `on_click()` so that events can't be fetched again until the button event is handled. This prevents fetching the events too often by repeated presses of a button. Perhaps `self.no_update` isn't a good name since it's not very accurate. Perhaps `self.no_event_update` or `self.no_event_fetch` would be clearer.

Two, if `button_refresh` is pushed we want to do (1) and (2), since we want to fetch events and then refresh the module to reflect any changes. However, this brings the crash problem back. If we fetch events too often in a short period of time (by pressing `button_refresh` many times quickly), the module will crash. So what I do to fix this case is, I use `self.last_update` in `_get_events()` to save the last time the module was updated. When `button_refresh` is pressed, we check how long it's been since the last event fetch. If it's been more than 1 second since the last time we fetched events, we allow them to be fetched again. This keeps the events from being fetched too often, which causes the crash.

I think your suggestion for handling url opening is right. It would make more sense to handle it in `on_click()`, but it won't remove the need for `self.no_update` because we still need to decouple refreshing the module from fetching events.

I'm struggling to find an alternate way to accomplish what this class does.

At first I was thinking of a simple `@property` method which raised the needed exception buts since we need to be able to access the `fallback` property on both the class and instance, that doesn't work.

That lead me to thinking about implementing this as a descriptor instead.

```python
class FallbackFunctionDescriptor:
    fallback_fn = None

    def __init__(self, fallback_fn=None):
        self.fallback_fn = None
    def __get__(self, instance, owner):
        ...
```

While it's still kind of messy, I think it's a little cleaner than raising the exception in `__getattr__` and it causes the exception to be raised at the moment that `c.fallback` is accessed, rather than my understanding of the current behavior which waits until an attribute on `c.fallback` is accessed.

Thoughts?

This PR changes this: for some errors it will still return failed Deferreds, but other errors might be raised immediately as direct exceptions. Hence all these changes in testing code.

This actually changes current API and potentially may break some existing code. (inlineCallbacks-based user code won't be affected). 

Previous behavior can be easily saved by returning `defer.fail(...)` instead of raising, but it looks ugly.

What is happening is that two separate threads are attempting to claim two locks in the reverse order. Thread 1 claims lock A and then tries to get lock B, meanwhile thread 2 claims lock B and then tries to get lock A. They will both then wait forever. This is a deadlock.

In our case lock A is an object lock on a specific `GangaObject` and lock B is the lock on the registry.

AI_MAX_ALLOC exists because Valgrind and AddressSanitizer have a maximum allocation size and exceeding it makes `new` return `nullptr` which breaks the normal rules. Changing this check breaks running under asan/valgrind. It's not possible to check every return value of `new` against `nullptr` and it would cause warnings from other tools since it can't happen in normal operation. However the value of AI_MAX_ALLOC could be increased or even moved to config.h.

All options are "batch_options" here, except the `pipeline_options` which is an object to pass to each pipeline. So, adding a generic `batch_options` does not follow the API pattern here. It would be better to have a keyword argument like `rate` or `frequency` or `sleep`, with a default value of `1`.

So, the kwarg would be (for example) `rate=1`. 

Then, the try/except for the rate is also a bit non-idiomatic. Once you set a sensible default, like `rate=1`, then you should do the checking in the `__init__` and not let bad values exist, only to be discovered in the `run` method.

if not isinstance(rate, (int, float)):

self.rate = rate

I'm of two minds about how to implement this. One thing we could do is have a fixed list of calendar types that are considered valid, even if unsupported, adding a class attribute like this:

```python
# List taken from https://www.unicode.org/repos/cldr/tags/latest/common/bcp47/calendar.xml
VALID_RSCALES = {'GREGORIAN', 'GREGORY',
                 'BUDDHIST', 'CHINESE', 'COPTIC', 'DANGI', ... }
```

Then change this check to:
```python
if rscale is not None:
    rscale = rscale.upper()
    if rscale in self.VALID_RSCALES:
        if rscale not in {'GREGORY', 'GREGORIAN'}:
            raise NotImplementedError('Non-gregorian calendar '
                                      '{} not supported.'.format(rscale))
    else:
        raise ValueError('Invalid RSCALE component: {}'.format(rscale))
```

Note that I also support lower and mixed-case calendar names there with the `upper` call. That said, an alternative to using string comparison at all is to simply have an enum-type class that enumerates the supported calendars. That seems like it might have some advantages here, but depending on the implementation it might make it harder for people to extend `dateutil` to support other calendars. String comparison seems like a good start.

The reason it was removed is that while there are other possibly complicated arguments to other `az` commands, none of them contain an inline example. The `--action` arguments have these as well and should be similarly removed.

If the documentation of the argument is not intuitive enough to provide an understanding without an example, that is a failing of the documentation. To me the format makes it clear what style of input is expected, but the lack of definition for METRIC, THRESHOLD, and PERIOD is what makes the example necessary. Additionally having an inline example is misleading - it may lead users to make _assumptions_ about the availability or format of these values which could be wrong.

Instead the `long-summary` here should include definitions of the various values and be structured similarly to:

> For conditions, metrics and appropriate threshold types can be retrieved with the `az monitor metrics` commands. The period for a condition is written as a timestamp in `XXhXXmXXs` format.

This, along with the existing examples, should be enough to provide adequate context.

This test is actually misleading as it should be `self.prefix, 'some_pkg-1.0.0-0`, not the meta.  Sorry for writing it that way.  `load_meta` requires a dist string, not the meta data since it actually loads the meta data given a string when used, but in this case it's mocked out inside `generate_mock_check` and it always returns `self.meta` without checking what was passed in.

Looking through the commit log associated with `generate_mock_check`, I see 2099eb7721263b168b56f60b4c67805d4fa2363b that changed the behavior.  Additional detail not captured in that commit log (that should have been) are that the original assumption was that by this point I had a prefix string and a dictionary representing a package.  That was wrong, so the test was adjusted, but not the parameters passed in to `ensure_write`.  Since everything was mocked, it doesn't make much difference other than to confuse folks who come along behind me.

---

Was there anything else in this code that was confusing.  Reading through it I can see the following steps:

* Pass out to a context manager which
  * Mocks `can_open_all_files_in_prefix` and sets it to return `True`
  * Mocks `load_meta` and sets it to always return `self.meta` regardless of what it is provided
  * `yield`s back
* Calls `ensure_write` which only calls those two functions which now have the mocked behavior described above

I purposely try to keep test cases very small and compact and the implementation of the function under test is likewise small (it calls two functions and throws an exception if the second returns `False`) to make it easier to understand.  Passing in invalid parameters definitely didn't help here, but I would love some ideas on how I could have made this more approachable.

The client may talk with Observers only, and not with Validators. Let's assume the client sends request X to Observer1 which has pool state X1 (this state can be used to validate state proofs). Then it sends request to Observer2 which has an *earlier* state X2 with different values of BLS keys (this state can be used to validate state proofs). So, the client needs to be able to get an old state of the pool to validate BLS sigs. It can easily be done using State trie, that's why I say that we need Pool State Trie for clients (sdk side).

So looking at this I noticed a problem I hadn't seen when I reviewed the extension API code into our fork, that the need to provide config values for `extension_directory` and `frontend_content` isn't actually really there and looks like redundant. Both values can be calculated from the entrypoint object that is returned when we load the `redash.extensions` entrypoints.

- `extension_directory` -- is the filesystem path of the package `redash_stmo.datasource_link`
- `frontend_content` -- has no need to be defined individually per extension and should instead be consistent for every extension

So I believe we should simplify the structure of an extension further:

#### An extension is a Python module or package which may or may not have a `bundle` subdirectory, and is defined in the `redash.extensions` entrypoint.

The code that loads the bundles in [bundle-extensions](https://github.com/mozilla/redash/blob/9424dadcc7c0e13d9f094b18914615bae756dd92/bin/bundle-extensions#L25-L30) should calculate the original source of the extension frontend files from the entry in the `redash.extensions` entrypoint and use the name of the loaded entrypoint (e.g. `datasource_link`) as the subdirectory in `client/app/extensions`.

That basically would remove the need for the `webpack.bundles` entrypoint again and consolidate frontend and backend into one location.

I think this line is too restrictive.

As an example, using CeleryExecutor but no workers, this DAG is almost immediately marked as failed (I had it on hand from testing a different issue):
https://gist.github.com/jlowin/4f0c88b80f35ac45862c6222d153abcf

I think what happens is that the first set of tasks are loaded and sent to the Executor, so their states are set to RUNNING even though there are no celery workers. The next time through the loop, the scheduler gets to this line and loads up the one remaining task -- one that is also downstream from all the RUNNING tasks and therefore can't run yet. It evaluates it, finds it can't run, and says the DAG is deadlocked because `could_not_run == available_tasks`. 

So I think we need to account for the fact that tasks may be running or in transit to the executor before moving on to deadlock assumption.
